title: Before It Disappears
opening: There comes a moment when a place no longer belongs only to the present. It begins to exist in two times at once, as something still lived, and something already becoming memory.

Before It Disappears follows this quiet transformation through my village of Joun, where I returned after years of living in Beirut. It is less concerned with disappearance itself than with the strange experience of witnessing it while life continues. Children still play in the streets, flowers return with the seasons, families gather, old men sit outside their homes. Nothing has stopped. And yet something has already begun to slip away.

Over the years, wars, migration, economic collapse, and the slow movement of time have altered the village and the lives around it. Houses empty, generations leave, familiar rhythms become quieter. What changes is not only the place itself, but the way it is inhabited, remembered, and carried by those who remain.

These photographs do not attempt to preserve the village against time or to mourn it before it is gone. They remain close to what is still here, attentive to the fragile coexistence of presence and disappearance. They ask what it means to love a place while knowing it cannot remain as it is.

sequence.cap: Kids running and playing in one of Joun’s old neighborhoods. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.

A grandfather and his grandson rest their hands on a steel railing at dusk, talking to each other in one of Joun’s neighborhoods, with the village’s mountains stretching out in front of them. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A grandfather holds his grandson near their home in one of the oldest village alleys, 2024.
An old man is watching two kids run through a narrow street in the village of Joun, as a white cat passes by. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2023.
Salam, one of the oldest residents of the village, walks past the entrance of his house with cracked concrete and peeling walls, holding a grapefruit. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2023.
An old man, Youssef — 91, one of the oldest people in Joun — stands in his rusty kitchen, waiting for the Arabic coffee pot to boil. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A portrait of Youssef — 91, among the oldest residents of Joun. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A man harvesting mulberries, almost entirely concealed by the thick branches. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
An old man, Salam — 82 and one of the oldest people in Joun — contemplates the green landscape. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
An elderly man walks along a stone wall as the photographer’s shadow falls beside him in Joun. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
Salam, one of the oldest residents of the village, pauses during a walk as the neighbor’s dog sits nearby. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.

fragment: Places survive within us after they have changed. People remain after they have gone.

There are moments when time slips and what was once returns to you so vividly, with such detail, that it unsettles you. Was it ever real, or only a dream? The question is not the point. The point is the returning, the fact that something in you still receives it, still trembles at it, still opens when every logic says to close.

sequence.cap: A stray cat sits inside an abandoned house in Joun, near a staircase that leads to an orange tree with fallen fruit scattered on the ground. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A locked wooden door in one of Joun’s old neighborhoods, its surface worn and covered with scribbles, and its rusted chains marking years of abandonment. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A boy points a toy gun while standing on a cracked street in Joun. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.
A cat sits on the edge of a wall at the entrance of an abandoned house in Joun. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.

fragment: Beauty and difficulty do not resolve one another. Innocence and violence, tenderness and destruction, play and seriousness can appear within the same frame.

sequence.cap: Salam — 82, one of the oldest people in Joun — walks down a village road at sunset, passing a tall pine tree and an old abandoned stone house. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
An old abandoned house in Joun, with a large pine tree growing in front of its entrance. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
Two elderly men, one handing grapefruits to the other, 2023.
Reda carries a basin of orange blossom flowers after harvesting while his uncle Khalil waits on the hood of his car beside a blooming tree. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
Kids running and playing in one of Joun’s old neighborhoods. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2023.
Kids running and playing in one of Joun’s old neighborhoods. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A boy tends to his sheep among olive trees in the hills surrounding Joun. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A boy plays alone with a soccer ball in an open space as clouds gather over the hills at dusk. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
Youssef — 91, one of the oldest men in Joun — looks toward an abandoned house that once belonged to a close friend who left the village during the civil war. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
An overgrown jasmine plant climbing an old, rustic wall in Joun, its tangled branches leaning to the right as soft, shimmering light falls across the surface. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A portrait of Maher, a local man, in the streets of Joun. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.
A bougainvillea tree grows in full bloom at the entrance of an abandoned house in Joun. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.

fragment: There is a method for growing rhubarb called forcing. The plant is raised in total darkness, tended only in brief intervals of candlelight. It thrives by reaching toward an absent sun, stretching so fast its growth can be heard. Forcing can weaken the crop, but when done carefully, it yields something sweeter.
I am not making an allegory. I am just saying that some things grow toward what they cannot see, and the growing is not separate from the sweetness.

sequence.cap: A covered, rusted car sits in a weathered garage in Joun, surrounded by old walls and overgrown plants. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.
A long road winds past olive trees and white wildflowers at the edge of Joun’s pine forest. Once crowded with people and traffic, the road now lies quiet. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2025.

sequence.title: Before It Disappears
sequence.cap: Before It Disappears — a film. Joun, Lebanon.

sequence.title: Before It Disappears, Part Two
sequence.cap: Before It Disappears, Part Two — a film. Joun, Lebanon.

fragment: Perhaps love slips through our hands not because it is absent, but because we are not there. To love another person, a place, or life itself may mean remaining before it without retreat, allowing it to exist beyond our need to name it.

sequence.cap: A view of Joun on a foggy evening, seen from one of the village rooftops. The town of Joun, Lebanon, 2024.

closing: If Colored Air emerged from a renewed attentiveness to the ordinary, Before It Disappears turns toward time itself. It is not a farewell, but a way of staying with what continues to exist while quietly becoming memory.
